content.body: Industrial chemistry applies chemical principles at large scale to produce commercially important materials efficiently and sustainably.

content.title: Contact Process (H₂SO₄)
content.body:
2SO₂ + O₂ ⇌ 2SO₃  (V₂O₅ catalyst, 450°C, 1-2 atm)
SO₃ + H₂SO₄ → H₂S₂O₇ (oleum)
H₂S₂O₇ + H₂O → 2H₂SO₄

Why not absorb SO₃ directly in water? Produces dangerous acid mist
Sulphuric acid: fertilisers, detergents, batteries, drugs

content.title: Haber Process (NH₃) and Ostwald Process (HNO₃)
content.body:
N₂+3H₂⇌2NH₃ (Fe, 450°C, 200atm)

Ostwald: NH₃→HNO₃
4NH₃+5O₂→4NO+6H₂O (Pt/Rh catalyst, 900°C)
2NO+O₂→2NO₂
3NO₂+H₂O→2HNO₃+NO

Applications: fertilisers (ammonium nitrate, urea), explosives

Economic and environmental considerations:
• Yield vs rate compromise (Haber)
• Atom economy: reduce waste
• Energy efficiency: heat exchange
• Pollution control: SO₂ scrubbers, catalytic converters
• Raw materials: cost, availability, renewability

questions.question: The Contact Process produces
questions.explanation: The Contact Process is the industrial method for producing sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄) from sulphur dioxide.

questions.question: In the Contact Process, SO₃ is not absorbed directly in water because
options: it produces dangerous sulphuric acid mist
questions.answer: it produces dangerous sulphuric acid mist
questions.explanation: Direct absorption of SO₃ in water produces a dangerous mist of fine H₂SO₄ droplets. Oleum is used instead.

questions.question: V₂O₅ in the Contact Process acts as
questions.explanation: Vanadium(V) oxide (V₂O₅) is the catalyst for 2SO₂+O₂→2SO₃.

questions.question: The Haber Process produces
questions.explanation: The Haber Process: N₂+3H₂→2NH₃ using Fe catalyst at 450°C and 200 atm.

questions.question: The Ostwald Process converts ammonia to
questions.explanation: Ostwald: oxidises NH₃ to NO, then NO₂, then dissolves in water to give HNO₃.

questions.question: Atom economy encourages chemists to
options: design reactions where more of the reactant atoms end up in the desired product
questions.answer: design reactions where more of the reactant atoms end up in the desired product
questions.explanation: High atom economy means less waste — important economically (raw materials) and environmentally.

questions.question: Catalytic converters in cars convert
options: CO and NOₓ to CO₂ and N₂
questions.answer: CO and NOₓ to CO₂ and N₂
questions.explanation: Pt/Rh/Pd catalysts oxidise CO→CO₂, hydrocarbons→CO₂+H₂O, and reduce NOₓ→N₂.

questions.question: Why is 450°C used in the Haber Process rather than a lower temperature?
options: Lower temperature gives too slow a reaction rate despite higher equilibrium yield
questions.answer: Lower temperature gives too slow a reaction rate despite higher equilibrium yield
questions.explanation: Compromise: very low T gives high yield but negligible rate. 450°C balances acceptable yield (~15%) with industrial rate.

questions.question: Nitrogen for the Haber Process comes from
questions.explanation: Liquid air is fractionally distilled — N₂ (bp -196°C) boils off separately from O₂ (bp -183°C).

questions.question: Sulphuric acid is described as the world's most important industrial chemical because
options: it is used in making fertilisers, detergents, drugs, explosives, batteries and many other products
questions.answer: it is used in making fertilisers, detergents, drugs, explosives, batteries and many other products
questions.explanation: H₂SO₄ is a starting material or reagent in producing an enormous range of industrial products.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body:
Electrochemical cells:
Galvanic cell: spontaneous redox → electrical energy
Electrolytic cell: electrical energy → non-spontaneous redox

Standard electrode potential E°:
More positive E° = better oxidising agent (reduction preferred)
Cell EMF = E°(cathode) - E°(anode)
Spontaneous if EMF > 0

content.title: Electroplating and Refining
content.body:
Electroplating (copper):
Cathode: object to be plated (Cu²⁺+2e⁻ → Cu)
Anode: copper (Cu → Cu²⁺+2e⁻)
Electrolyte: copper sulphate solution

Copper refining:
Impure Cu anode dissolves, pure Cu deposits on cathode
Anode sludge: precious metals (Ag, Au) collect below anode

content.title: Batteries and Fuel Cells
content.body:
Primary (non-rechargeable): Zn-MnO₂ (dry cell)
Secondary (rechargeable): Lead-acid (car battery), Li-ion (phones)
Fuel cell: H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O, releasing electrical energy
H₂ fuel cell: no emissions (only water), high efficiency
Challenge: H₂ storage and production

questions.question: In a galvanic cell, chemical energy converts to
questions.explanation: Galvanic (voltaic) cell: spontaneous redox reaction drives electrons through external circuit → electrical energy.

questions.question: Standard electrode potential E° measures
options: tendency to be reduced (gain electrons) relative to standard hydrogen electrode
questions.answer: tendency to be reduced (gain electrons) relative to standard hydrogen electrode
questions.explanation: More positive E°: more readily reduced (stronger oxidising agent). Used to predict cell reactions.

questions.question: Cell EMF =
questions.explanation: EMF = E°(right/cathode) - E°(left/anode). Positive EMF = spontaneous reaction.

questions.question: In electroplating, the object to be plated is the
questions.explanation: The object receives metal ions from solution (reduction) at the cathode (negative electrode).

questions.question: Lead-acid car battery is a secondary cell because
options: it can be recharged by reversing the reaction
questions.answer: it can be recharged by reversing the reaction
questions.explanation: Secondary cells are rechargeable — applying external EMF reverses the chemical reaction, restoring the battery.

questions.question: Hydrogen fuel cells produce as their only emission
questions.explanation: H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O. The only product is water — zero harmful emissions if H₂ produced from renewable sources.

questions.question: Why is copper refined electrolytically?
options: To obtain very high purity needed for electrical conductors
questions.answer: To obtain very high purity needed for electrical conductors
questions.explanation: Electrical conductors need 99.99% pure copper — impurities dramatically reduce conductivity.

questions.question: The anode in a galvanic cell
questions.explanation: In galvanic cell: anode = negative electrode where oxidation occurs (loses electrons).

questions.question: Corrosion of iron (rusting) is
options: an electrochemical process where iron is oxidised
questions.answer: an electrochemical process where iron is oxidised
questions.explanation: Rusting: Fe → Fe²⁺ (oxidation at anode areas). Oxygen and water act as cathodic areas. Electrochemical corrosion cell forms.

questions.question: Zinc coating (galvanising) protects iron because
options: zinc is a sacrificial anode — more reactive so oxidises preferentially instead of iron
questions.answer: zinc is a sacrificial anode — more reactive so oxidises preferentially instead of iron
questions.explanation: Zinc is more reactive than iron — it oxidises (corrodes) preferentially, protecting the iron underneath.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: Modern chemistry has produced many novel materials with unique properties. Nanotechnology works at the scale of atoms and molecules (1-100 nanometres).

content.title: Smart Materials
content.body:
Shape memory alloys (e.g. Nitinol): return to original shape when heated
Applications: spectacle frames, stents, actuators

Photochromic materials: darken in UV, clear indoors
Applications: transition lenses

Thermochromic materials: change colour with temperature
Applications: temperature-sensitive labels

Piezoelectric materials: generate electricity under pressure
Applications: sensors, speakers, sonar

content.title: Nanomaterials
content.body:
Carbon nanotubes: cylindrical, extremely strong, conduct electricity
Strength: 100× steel at fraction of weight
Applications: aerospace, electronics, drug delivery

Graphene: single atom layer of carbon
Hardest known material, excellent conductor
Applications: flexible screens, water filters

Nanoparticles in medicine: drug delivery to specific cells
Nanoparticles in sunscreen: TiO₂ blocks UV without white residue

Why nano is different:
High surface area to volume ratio
Quantum effects become significant
Unique optical, electrical, magnetic properties
Gold nanoparticles: red/purple (not gold coloured)
Silver nanoparticles: antibacterial

questions.question: Nanotechnology works at the scale of
questions.explanation: Nanotechnology manipulates matter at 1-100 nanometres — the scale of individual molecules and atomic clusters.

questions.question: Shape memory alloys are special because
options: they return to original shape when heated
questions.answer: they return to original shape when heated
questions.explanation: Shape memory alloys (like Nitinol) deform at low temperature but snap back to their memorised shape when warmed.

questions.question: Carbon nanotubes are extremely strong due to
options: sp² carbon bonds in cylindrical arrangement
questions.answer: sp² carbon bonds in cylindrical arrangement
questions.explanation: The covalent C-C bonds in the hexagonal nanotube lattice make them stronger than steel at a fraction of the weight.

questions.question: Graphene is a single layer of carbon atoms arranged in
questions.explanation: Graphene: one atom thick layer of carbon in hexagonal arrangement — 2D allotrope of carbon.

questions.question: Why do nanomaterials have different properties from bulk material?
options: Very high surface area:volume ratio and quantum effects become significant
questions.answer: Very high surface area:volume ratio and quantum effects become significant
questions.explanation: At nanoscale: huge SA:Vol ratio (more surface reactions), and quantum mechanical effects alter electronic/optical properties.

questions.question: Gold nanoparticles appear red/purple, not gold, because
options: quantum effects alter optical properties at nanoscale
questions.answer: quantum effects alter optical properties at nanoscale
questions.explanation: At nanoscale, gold nanoparticles have plasmon resonance that absorbs different wavelengths than bulk gold.

questions.question: TiO₂ nanoparticles in sunscreen
options: block UV without visible white residue
questions.answer: block UV without visible white residue
questions.explanation: Nanoparticle TiO₂ is transparent to visible light but still absorbs UV — gives effective UV protection without the white paste.

questions.question: Piezoelectric materials convert
options: pressure to electrical signals (and vice versa)
questions.answer: pressure to electrical signals (and vice versa)
questions.explanation: Piezoelectric effect: mechanical pressure → electric signal. Used in sensors, lighters, sonar, microphones.

questions.question: The main concern about nanomaterials for health and environment is
options: their behaviour in biological systems is poorly understood — may be toxic
questions.answer: their behaviour in biological systems is poorly understood — may be toxic
questions.explanation: Nanoparticles are small enough to penetrate cell membranes and cross the blood-brain barrier. Long-term effects not fully known.

questions.question: Thermochromic materials are used in
options: temperature-sensitive indicators that change colour with temperature
questions.answer: temperature-sensitive indicators that change colour with temperature
questions.explanation: Thermochromic inks change colour at specific temperatures — used on food packaging, baby bottles, temperature strips.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Industrial Chemistry. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Industrial Chemistry concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Industrial Chemistry builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 4.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Industrial Chemistry. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 4.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Industrial Chemistry. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Industrial Chemistry include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Industrial Chemistry. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).

Learning Objectives

Common examination questions on Industrial Chemistry include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Industrial Chemistry. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).
